additional_information.other_condition_description: Chronic insomnia and nightmares related to trauma. Client reports difficulty both falling and staying asleep for the past 3+ years, with sleep often limited to 3–4 hours per night in broken segments. Nightmares typically involve re-experiencing the motor vehicle accident from 2022 and earlier childhood emotional abuse. Sleep disruption significantly worsens daytime anxiety, irritability, and concentration. Client has tried over-the-counter sleep aids (melatonin, diphenhydramine) with limited benefit and experienced grogginess the next day. No current prescription sleep medication. Sleep hygiene is inconsistent due to shift work; client often naps after work and then lies awake for hours at night. Client also reports hypervigilance at night, frequently waking to check that doors are locked and scanning for sounds. This condition has been present for more than 6 months and is still active in the last 6 months but is considered secondary to the primary PTSD/trauma-related diagnosis.

current_risk_assessment.additional_details_regarding_current_ris:
Client reports intermittent passive suicidal ideation over the past two years, typically occurring during periods of high stress, poor sleep, and increased PTSD symptoms. Passive thoughts are described as "wishing I wouldn't wake up" or thinking "it would be easier if I just disappeared," but client denies any current active plan, intent, or preparatory behaviors. Client consistently identifies strong protective factors including responsibility for their 8-year-old daughter, close relationship with their sister, and fear of the impact on family if they were to die by suicide.

History of self-harm: Client reports one episode of superficial cutting on the left forearm approximately 18 months ago during a period of intense emotional distress following an argument with their ex-partner. The client used a clean razor blade from a shaving kit, created several shallow scratches that did not require medical attention, and stopped when they saw blood and became frightened. Client disposed of the razor after this incident and has not engaged in cutting or other self-injurious behavior since. Client denies any history of suicide attempts, overdoses, or hospitalizations for suicidality.

Client denies any history of violence toward others, including physical fights, assaults, or threats with weapons. When angry, client tends to withdraw, cry, or engage in verbal arguments rather than physical aggression. No access to firearms in the home; client states there are kitchen knives and basic tools only. Client lives with a roommate who is aware of some of the client's mental health struggles and can be contacted in a crisis.

Currently, client rates their safety as 7/10 (10 = completely safe) and is willing to reach out to their sister, roommate, or crisis line if suicidal thoughts intensify. Client has previously used a crisis line once about a year ago and found it helpful. Client is open to developing a more formal safety plan as part of treatment and agrees to inform providers if passive thoughts shift toward more active planning or intent.

history_of_addictionsubstance_use.if_yes_type_of_substance_length_of_use_a:
Client reports a history of moderate alcohol use and brief experimentation with cannabis in late adolescence/early adulthood, but no current substance use and no history suggestive of a substance use disorder.

Alcohol: Began drinking socially around age 19, primarily on weekends. From approximately ages 22–28, client drank 2–4 beers or mixed drinks on Friday and Saturday nights, occasionally more during special events (e.g., birthdays, holidays). There were a few instances of binge drinking in their mid-20s that led to hangovers and one argument with a partner but no blackouts, DUIs, or legal consequences. Over the past three years, particularly since the motor vehicle accident and onset of more severe PTSD symptoms, client noticed that even small amounts of alcohol worsened sleep and anxiety the next day. As a result, client made a conscious decision to significantly reduce alcohol intake. Client reports last alcoholic drink was a single beer at a family barbecue approximately 4 months ago. Since then, client has chosen to remain abstinent to support mental health treatment and meet program requirements.

Cannabis: Client experimented with cannabis a handful of times between ages 19–21, typically in social settings with friends. Use was sporadic (less than once per month) and discontinued entirely by age 22 because it increased feelings of paranoia and heart racing. No cannabis use in the past several years.

Other substances: Client denies any history of cocaine, methamphetamine, opioids, hallucinogens, or misuse of prescription medications. No history of rehabilitation programs, detox, or mandated substance use treatment. Client has occasionally used over-the-counter pain relievers (ibuprofen, acetaminophen) as directed for chronic neck and back pain following the accident but denies overuse or misuse.

Treatment history: Client has not participated in any formal substance use treatment programs, as their use never reached a level they or prior providers considered an addiction. Psychoeducation from primary care regarding the impact of alcohol on sleep and mood was provided in 2023, and client voluntarily reduced intake at that time.

Client understands and accepts the program requirement to be free of substance use for at least 30 days prior to admission. Based on self-report and collateral from sister, client has been abstinent from alcohol and all non-prescribed substances for more than 90 days at the time of this referral.

required_documents.other_required_document_description: Attached: 1) Trauma-focused psychological assessment report from Dr. Melissa Chan, Registered Psychologist, dated 2024-09-30, including DSM-5 diagnosis of PTSD and Major Depressive Disorder, moderate. 2) Discharge summary from outpatient physiotherapy following motor vehicle accident (2023-04-15 to 2023-10-20), outlining ongoing chronic neck and upper back pain. 3) Recent primary care physician note from Dr. Jonathan Patel dated 2024-10-10 summarizing current medications, sleep concerns, and recommendation for specialized trauma program referral.

notes_and_signature.notes:
Client is a 34-year-old single parent referred for specialized trauma-focused mental health services due to persistent PTSD symptoms, co-occurring anxiety and depression, and significant functional impairment in daily life and work. Client was involved in a serious motor vehicle accident in March 2022 in which their vehicle was rear-ended at high speed. Although there were no life-threatening physical injuries, client sustained whiplash and ongoing neck/upper back pain. Since the accident, client reports frequent intrusive memories, nightmares, avoidance of driving on highways, hypervigilance, exaggerated startle response, and difficulty concentrating at work. Symptoms have persisted despite regular outpatient counseling and medication management through primary care.

In addition to the accident, client disclosed a history of emotional abuse and chronic criticism in childhood, particularly from a highly demanding and unpredictable father. Client minimizes these experiences but describes long-standing feelings of "never being good enough" and a tendency to overwork and over-please others. These early experiences appear to contribute to current self-esteem issues, shame, and difficulty setting boundaries in relationships.

Functionally, client works part-time as a medical office assistant at a family practice clinic. They have reduced from full-time to 0.6 FTE over the past year due to increased fatigue, sleep disturbance, and difficulty managing anxiety in busy or chaotic environments. Employer has been somewhat supportive but has noted increased absenteeism and difficulty with multitasking under pressure. Client is worried about job security and financial stability, particularly as they are the primary caregiver for their 8-year-old daughter. Client shares 50/50 custody with ex-partner; co-parenting relationship is generally civil but can be tense around scheduling and finances.

Client has participated in short-term CBT-based counseling through an Employee Assistance Program (6 sessions in 2023) and 12 sessions with a community counselor focused on coping strategies and basic grounding skills. While client found these supports somewhat helpful, symptoms remain moderate to severe and have plateaued. Primary care physician believes client would benefit from a more intensive, structured, trauma-informed program that can address both PTSD and associated mood/anxiety symptoms, as well as provide skills for emotion regulation, distress tolerance, and interpersonal effectiveness.

Client is highly motivated for treatment, expresses insight into the impact of trauma on current functioning, and is willing to engage in group work, psychoeducation, and individual therapy components. Client has reliable transportation (avoids highways but can use surface streets or public transit) and stable housing (shares a two-bedroom apartment with a long-term roommate who is supportive and aware of the referral). No current substance use and no active safety concerns requiring inpatient care at this time.

Overall, this referral is being made to support client in stabilizing PTSD and depressive symptoms, improving sleep, increasing coping skills, and ultimately enhancing their ability to function consistently at work and at home as a parent. Program with strong trauma focus, psychoeducation, and skills-based interventions (e.g., CBT, DBT-informed skills, grounding, and possibly EMDR or other trauma processing approaches) is recommended.
